summary: Gruber discusses Apple's pattern of building features into its platforms that were originally third-party innovations, prompted by an MKBHD video on the topic. He argues this is an evergreen dynamic not specific to Apple: platform makers inevitably absorb the best ideas from smaller companies. Using examples from Watson/Sherlock to AirTags/Tile, he contends that smart third-party developers survive by targeting power users and going beyond what the platform offers, while companies like Tile whose products are inherently simple have little room to differentiate. He concludes that innovators should design with the expectation that good ideas will eventually be built in.
key_points: Platform absorption of third-party innovations is an evergreen pattern, not unique to Apple — good ideas that should be built in eventually will be
Third-party utilities like LaunchBar, Alfred, and Raycast survive Spotlight by targeting power users and doing more than the built-in version
Tile's problem is that location tags are inherently simple with no room for an enthusiast tier, making Apple's entry essentially fatal to their market
The footnote highlights a key Mac vs iOS distinction: the Mac allows system-wide third-party utilities while iOS does not, limiting the survival strategies available on mobile
money_quotes: Smaller companies make products that build upon, or fill gaps within, platforms from larger companies. The best of those ideas — ideas that truly would be better 'built in', eventually do get built in.
The trick to remaining useful as a Spotlight-like utility after Apple built Spotlight into the OS is to do more.
Spotlight is designed for everyone to use — it's simple and only does simple things. LaunchBar / Alfred / Raycast keep the simple things simple but also make complex things possible.
If the answer to the question 'Would this add-on be better, and be useful to many users, if it were built into the system?' is yes, you should expect it to be built into the system sooner or later.
If you have a good idea for a third-party product on a big platform, you need to expect that the platform maker will eventually use your idea.
If your idea doesn't have room for an enthusiast/professional/power user tier — hello, Tile — again, maybe it wasn't that great an idea in the first place.
You can say that's a shame, but it's hard for me to buy that Tile has been wronged.
Everything is an innovation at some point. But things that should be built in eventually will be built in, and innovators need to design with that in mind.
key_insight: Third-party innovators on major platforms should expect their best ideas to be absorbed and plan to survive by serving power users beyond what the platform maker is willing to offer.
